title: Modeling the Quantum Photon Statistics in Hybrid Light-Matter Integrated Circuits
authors: Mathias Van Regemortel, Vincenzo Ardizzone, Eugenio Maggiolini, Armando Rastelli, Daniele Sanvitto, Thomas Van Vaerenbergh
abstract: Strong light-matter coupling between a guided electromagnetic mode and an excitonic semiconductor transition gives rise to exciton-polaritons with optical nonlinearities far exceeding those of conventional photonic platforms. Utilizing these nonlinearities in the few-particle regime, where quantum signatures such as photon antibunching, sub-Poissonian statistics and non-trivial inter-mode correlations become accessible, is a central goal of integrated quantum photonics. Yet, a quantitative theoretical framework connecting realistic waveguide parameters to measurable non-classical photonic output is absent. Here, we present a comprehensive framework for predicting and benchmarking quantum photon statistics in polaritonic integrated circuits, using state-of-the-art experimentally achieved device parameters for (Al)GaAs waveguide platforms. By mapping the pulsed nonlinear waveguide dynamics onto a bosonic quantum circuit representation that explicitly incorporates dissipation, we identify experimentally accessible quantum signatures across two circuit configurations: a single waveguide in a free-space interferometric configuration and a fully integrated multimode coupled-waveguide circuit. We further show that slow-light engineering of the polariton dispersion offers a practical route to amplifying the effective nonlinearity, pushing quantum signatures beyond Gaussian statistics.
summary: This paper presents a new theoretical framework for predicting non-classical photon statistics in hybrid (Al)GaAs polaritonic integrated circuits. It demonstrates how engineered features like slow-light dispersion and MZI configurations can amplify photon antibunching and bunching. The work provides a roadmap for achieving quantum-level light manipulation on-chip.
main_problem: The lack of a quantitative theoretical framework to connect realistic waveguide parameters to measurable non-classical photon statistics in hybrid light-matter integrated circuits.
main_result: The development of a comprehensive framework (qPIC) that predicts quantum signatures like photon antibunching and demonstrates that slow-light engineering can amplify nonlinearities to achieve non-Gaussian statistics.
method: Mapping pulsed nonlinear waveguide dynamics onto a bosonic quantum circuit representation using the Matrix Product State Density Operator (MPDO) framework and Kraus operators for dissipation.
model_or_system: Hybrid (Al)GaAs light-matter integrated circuits supporting exciton-polaritons, modeled via a Kerr-type Hamiltonian in both single-waveguide MZI and multi-mode coupled-waveguide architectures.
key_observables: Photon-number output statistics, second-order correlation function g(2)(0) (antibunching and bunching), signal intensity, and intensity-intensity correlation matrices.
important_parameters: Rabi splitting, exciton interaction constant, group velocity (v_g), Hopfield coefficients, and local oscillator phase.
main_assumptions: 1-D longitudinal wavevector approximation, pulse envelope invariance over time, and local Fock-space truncation.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows polariton dispersion and derived group velocity; Figure 2 depicts the MZI setup; Figure 7 illustrates the impact of losses on signal intensity and correlations; Figure 8 provides 2D maps of intensity and g(2) as a function of phase and mixing angle.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of quantifying quantum statistics in polaritonic circuits, presents a new theoretical framework (qPIC), analyzes single-waveguide MZI configurations, extends the model to integrated multi-mode circuits, and discusses the benefits of slow-light engineering and the impact of losses.
why_interesting: It provides a bridge between semiconductor device physics and quantum optics, offering a scalable framework for designing integrated quantum photonic circuits using nonlinear light-matter interactions.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: The experimental LP dispersion in an AlGaAs waveguide with GaAs quantum wells and derived quantities. (a) Photoluminscence of the dispersion of the guided polaritonic mode extracted from an input/output diffraction grating; vertical axis shows the energy of the polariton mode while the horizontal axis is the wavevector along the propagation direction. (b) Group velocity vg (blue solid, left axis) and exciton fraction |uk|2 (red dashed, right axis) obtained from the dispersion in (a).

Figure 2: The Mach-Zehnder interferometer setup for generating antibunched photon statistic. After the first 50 : 50 beam splitter (BS) coupler, one arm (the local oscillator) has a tunable phase shift ϕLO, while the other couples in the nonlinear waveguide. After the second coupler a HBT experiment is performed of the signal in the two arms, labeled with the mode index l = 0, 1.

Figure 3: Results for the out-coupled photon statistics of a BS with coupling θout = 0.2π, when modulating the MZI LO phase within the range ϕLO ∈{0, 2π} for an incoming pulse of |α|2 = 1 and 1 mm waveguide coupling at u2 k = 0.3 exciton fraction. (a) The varying output intensity across the two modes, on log-scale. (b)-(d) Intensity correlations, intra-mode (blue–orange, solid), cross-correlations (black, dot-dashed) and the coherent correlation for reference (gray, dotted), at the reported nonlinearities. Sub-Poissonian statistics, reaching g(2) ll < 0.5 at ℏg = 700 µeV µm2 is observed in the antisymmetric output arm around ϕLO/2π ≈0.45, just below ϕLO = π

Figure 4: The prototype architecture studied for the first experiments. It consists of L = 6 coupled waveguides, integrated in a 1 mm architecture consisting of D = 5 stacked layers of coupling gates. The signal is injected in waveguides l = 0 and l = 5 and the output signals of the waveguides are detected individually. It is assumed that all couplers are uniform across the circuit, but the circuit coupling J∆t and U∆t will rescale in function of the input wavevector k.

Figure 5: The simulation results of a circuit of L = 6 waveguides and depth D = 5, for experimentally reported exciton interaction strengths, for zero losses and φrel = 0.6π. (a) The output pulse intensities (pulse photon number) for ℏg = 10µeV µm2 (indistinguishable from ℏg = [50, 700] µeV µm2). (b) The expected intra-pulse intensity-intensity correlations. Strong antibunched photon statistics is witnessed at low output intensities. In all cases, there is sharp crossover from antibunching to bunching statistics around the low-intensity values k ≈[0.15, 0.27] µm−1
